ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Punjabi Sarson Ka Saag as a prepared dish; closest parent is mustard spinach raw ingredient. Prepared cooked dish with spices not represented in FoodOn.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers mustard leaves as a raw commodity; no specific code exists for the prepared Punjabi cooked saag dish. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept identified for Punjabi Sarson Ka Saag as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Punjabi Sarson Ka Saag is approximately 200 g, served as one bowl alongside makki di roti (maize flatbread) and a knob of butter or ghee.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Sarson (mustard leaves, cooked) and composite saag preparation
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods provides values for cooked mustard leaves (sarson) and spinach. Macro and micronutrient values for the composite saag dish are adapted from ICMR-NIN cooked mustard greens and spinach entries, adjusted for the addition of ghee (~5 g per 100 g serving) and minor spice additions (ginger, garlic, cumin). Energy and fat are upward-adjusted to reflect ghee content typical of restaurant preparation. Sodium reflects added salt in restaurant preparation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — leafy green vegetable dishes and cooked greens
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Punjabi Sarson Ka Saag in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 32 based on analogous cooked leafy green vegetable dishes (cooked spinach and mixed greens preparations) which consistently show GI values in the range 25–40 per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) International Tables of GI and GL. The high dietary fibre content (3.2 g/100 g) and low net carbohydrate content (6.8 g/100 g) support a low GI classification. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (32 × 6.8 × 2) / 100 = 4.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from added salt and ghee in restaurant preparation
hypertension.guidance: Restaurant-prepared Sarson Ka Saag contains approximately 320 mg sodium per 100 g (640 mg per standard 200 g serving), which contributes meaningfully to the 1500–2000 mg daily sodium target recommended for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced salt and minimal added ghee.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and high dietary fibre content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a low estimated GI of 32 and 3.2 g dietary fibre per 100 g, Sarson Ka Saag produces a modest glycemic load of approximately 4.4 per serving and is generally suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without excessive ghee or high-GI accompaniments such as white rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from ghee and beneficial micronutrients from mustard greens
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While mustard greens provide cardioprotective folate, potassium, vitamin C, and dietary fibre, the ghee used in restaurant preparation contributes approximately 3.2 g saturated fat per 100 g; individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate portion size and request preparation with minimal ghee or substitute with a plant-based oil.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Punjabi Sarson Ka Saag is a traditional North Indian cooked leafy green dish originating from the Punjab region of India and Pakistan. It is prepared by slow-cooking mustard greens (sarson) together with spinach, ginger, garlic, green chillies, and onion until tender, then blending or mashing the mixture to a thick, velvety consistency. A small amount of maize flour (makki atta) is often stirred in to thicken the saag, and the dish is finished with a generous tempering of ghee or butter. The flavour profile is earthy, slightly bitter from the mustard greens, aromatic from the ginger and garlic, and rich from the ghee. The texture is thick and smooth with some fibrous character. Sarson Ka Saag is traditionally served with makki di roti (unleavened maize flatbread) and is a staple winter dish across Punjab. Nutritionally, it is a rich source of dietary fibre (3.2 g/100 g), calcium (155 mg/100 g), iron (3.1 mg/100 g), vitamin A (210 mcg RAE/100 g), vitamin C (28 mg/100 g), and folate (45 mcg DFE/100 g). The dish has a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 32, making it suitable for blood glucose management. The primary clinical consideration is the sodium content (~320 mg/100 g) and saturated fat from ghee (~3.2 g/100 g) in restaurant preparations, which are relevant for hypertensive and cardiovascular patients. It is vegetarian, halal, and gluten-free when prepared without wheat-based thickeners.
common_user_queries: Is sarson ka saag good for diabetics?
What greens are in sarson ka saag?
How is sarson ka saag made?

ingredient_synonyms.sarson: mustard greens, mustard leaves, Brassica juncea leaves

confidence_reason: No exact match for Punjabi Sarson Ka Saag as a prepared restaurant dish exists in ICMR-NIN tables. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN cooked mustard greens and spinach entries with upward adjustment for ghee content typical of restaurant preparation. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database cooked leafy green references. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the adaptation and estimation involved.
notes: Nutrient values adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (cooked mustard leaves and spinach) with adjustment for approximately 5 g ghee per 100 g of prepared dish as typical in restaurant preparation. Vitamin B12 is 0.0 as this is a plant-based dish containing no animal-derived ingredients other than ghee, which contributes negligible B12; confirmed zero for practical purposes in a vegetarian leafy green preparation. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this food category. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for mustard greens or composite saag preparations. GI of 32 is an analogous estimate based on cooked leafy green vegetable dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct measurement for sarson ka saag was found. Sodium value of 320 mg/100 g reflects typical restaurant-level salting; home-prepared versions may have significantly lower sodium. The dish is served at Zaffron Tandoori, Boat Quay, Singapore (field survey source record GD003991). Price SGD 17.90.
